behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure is at module handoff (file discovery -> sort -> execution order), not a single pure function. REAL-INFRA: no real OS/network/subprocess behavior is required beyond local filesystem + DB wiring. MOCK-HIDE: a unit test of only the sorter could miss runner code that ignores sorted output when executing. LOWER-LEVEL-MISS-PROOF: Unit would miss a bug where files are sorted correctly but executed in insertion/discovery order by the runner pipeline. REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because behavior text only requires ordering by filename prefix, not real subprocess/network/OS timing behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run the runner against a temp migrations directory and test DB with out-of-order filenames (e.g., 001, 010, 002). Assert applied order recorded in _migrations (and/or side-effect table) is 001 -> 002 -> 010.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: idempotency depends on state handoff between metadata table reads and execution planner across runs. REAL-INFRA: no real network/subprocess requirement; local DB state is sufficient. MOCK-HIDE: unit tests of 'shouldRun' logic can pass while runner fails to persist/read _migrations correctly between runs. LOWER-LEVEL-MISS-PROOF: Unit would miss a bug where metadata write succeeds in one module but subsequent run reads from wrong schema/table and re-executes. REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because behavior text is about subsequent run semantics, not OS/process/network primitives.
behaviors.test_description: Execute runner twice on same DB with a migration that has observable side effect (insert row guarded only by runner idempotency). Assert second run applies zero new migrations and side effect count remains 1.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: correctness depends on real PostgreSQL transaction behavior on statement failure. MOCK-HIDE: fake DB/transaction stubs often simulate rollback perfectly and miss engine-specific failure semantics (DDL transactional behavior, error states aborting tx). MULTI-STEP/LLM-DEP: none. LOWER-LEVEL-MISS-PROOF: Integration with mocked transaction layer would miss a real Postgres case where first statement persists due to incorrect transaction scoping and runner still proceeds to next file. REJECTION-GATE: rejected Integration because behavior text explicitly requires automatic rollback of actual migration changes after statement failure, which is DB-engine behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Against real Postgres, run migrations where one file does a successful change then a failing statement; place another valid migration after it. Assert partial changes from failed migration are absent and later migration was not applied.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: requires separate processes and real DB concurrency/locking behavior. MOCK-HIDE: in-process fakes or serialized tests hide races, lock contention, and isolation anomalies. MULTI-STEP/LLM-DEP: none. LOWER-LEVEL-MISS-PROOF: Integration would miss a race where both processes pass 'not applied yet' check before either inserts metadata, causing duplicate execution. REJECTION-GATE: rejected Integration because behavior text explicitly says 'two instances ... at the same time', i.e., concurrent process boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Launch two runner processes simultaneously against same Postgres and same migration set; include a migration with unique side effect. Assert each migration appears once in _migrations and side effects are not duplicated.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the _migrations table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is initialization ordering across modules (bootstrap metadata -> apply files). REAL-INFRA: no subprocess/network/OS-specific behavior needed; local DB integration suffices. MOCK-HIDE: unit tests of 'ensureTable' alone miss runner sequencing bugs where first migration is attempted before metadata initialization. LOWER-LEVEL-MISS-PROOF: Unit would miss a bug where ensure step exists but is invoked after file execution starts. REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because behavior text concerns startup ordering on first run, not concurrent processes or real OS/subprocess effects.
behaviors.test_description: Run runner on empty DB with at least one migration. Assert _migrations exists before migration apply side effect is observed (or verify runner succeeds where first migration expects metadata tracking). Confirm metadata row is written for applied migration.
